  2. A Guide to Korean Food Categories

A Guide to Korean Food Categories

Understanding the Major Types of Korean Cuisine

Author

Korea & Japan Trip 2025

Published

October 1, 2025

Introduction

Korean cuisine is renowned for its bold flavors, emphasis on fermentation, and the principle of balance - between hot and cold, spicy and mild, meat and vegetables. This guide covers the major categories of Korean food you’ll encounter during your visit.

Kimchi & Banchan (Side Dishes)

Kimchi (김치)

Korea’s most iconic dish - fermented vegetables (usually napa cabbage) seasoned with gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes), garlic, ginger, and jeotgal (salted seafood). There are hundreds of varieties of kimchi made with different vegetables and seasonings.

Common types: - Baechu kimchi - traditional napa cabbage kimchi - Kkakdugi - cubed radish kimchi - Oi sobagi - cucumber kimchi - Nabak kimchi - water kimchi with radish and napa cabbage

Banchan (반찬)

Small side dishes served complimentary with every Korean meal. Can range from 3-4 dishes at casual restaurants to 10+ at traditional restaurants. Common banchan include seasoned spinach, bean sprouts, fishcake, japchae, and various types of kimchi.

Rice Dishes (Bap)

Bibimbap (비빔밥)

A bowl of warm white rice topped with vegetables, beef, a fried egg, and gochujang (red chili paste). Everything is mixed together before eating. Dolsot bibimbap is served in a hot stone bowl, creating a crispy rice crust at the bottom.

Kimbap (김밥)

Korean rice rolls wrapped in seaweed with various fillings like vegetables, egg, pickled radish, and sometimes meat or fish. Often called “Korean sushi” but has distinct flavors and is not vinegared.

Bokkeumbap (볶음밥)

Korean fried rice, often made with kimchi and other ingredients. Frequently served after Korean BBQ using the leftover meat and vegetables on the grill.

Noodle Dishes (Myeon)

Japchae (잡채)

Stir-fried sweet potato glass noodles with vegetables and sometimes beef, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. A popular dish for celebrations and holidays.

Naengmyeon (냉면)

Cold buckwheat noodles served in an icy broth or with spicy sauce. Particularly popular in summer and after Korean BBQ as a palate cleanser.

Jajangmyeon (자장면)

Korean-Chinese noodles with a thick black bean sauce. One of Korea’s most beloved comfort foods, often delivered as takeout.

Ramyeon (라면)

Korean instant noodles, but also served fresh at restaurants. Korean ramyeon tends to be spicier than Japanese ramen.

Stews & Soups

Jjigae (찌개) - Stews

Thick, hearty stews typically served bubbling in a clay pot or stone bowl.

Popular varieties: - Kimchi jjigae - kimchi stew with pork and tofu - Doenjang jjigae - fermented soybean paste stew with vegetables and tofu - Sundubu jjigae - soft tofu stew with vegetables, often spicy - Budae jjigae - “army base stew” with spam, hot dogs, ramen, and kimchi (originated after Korean War)

Guk & Tang (국 & 탕) - Soups

Lighter broths served with meals.

Common types: - Miyeokguk - seaweed soup (traditionally eaten on birthdays) - Samgyetang - ginseng chicken soup with whole young chicken stuffed with rice - Galbitang - beef short rib soup - Yukgaejang - spicy beef soup with vegetables - Seolleongtang - milky ox bone soup

Korean BBQ & Grilled Meats (Gui)

Bulgogi (불고기)

Thinly sliced marinated beef (or pork) grilled at the table. The marinade typically includes soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and pear juice, giving it a sweet and savory flavor.

Galbi (갈비)

Marinated beef or pork short ribs, grilled and cut into pieces. LA galbi refers to ribs cut across the bone in the American style.

Samgyeopsal (삼겹살)

Thick slices of pork belly grilled at the table, typically unseasoned. Wrapped in lettuce leaves with garlic, ssamjang (thick spicy paste), and other accompaniments.

Dakgalbi (닭갈비)

Spicy stir-fried chicken with vegetables, rice cakes, and sweet potatoes, cooked on a large griddle at the table.

Korean BBQ Etiquette

  • Meat is typically grilled at your table by you or the server
  • Wrap grilled meat in lettuce or perilla leaves with rice, garlic, and sauces (called ssam)
  • Use scissors to cut the meat
  • Don’t flip meat too often - let it cook properly on each side

Fried & Savory Foods

Jeon (전) / Buchimgae (부침개)

Korean savory pancakes made with various ingredients.

Popular varieties: - Pajeon - green onion pancake - Haemul pajeon - seafood and green onion pancake - Kimchijeon - kimchi pancake - Bindaetteok - mung bean pancake

Korean Fried Chicken (치킨)

Korea’s famous twice-fried chicken, known for its ultra-crispy coating. Often served with beer (called chimaek = chicken + maekju/beer).

Common styles: - Yangnyeom chicken - coated in sweet and spicy sauce - Ganjang chicken - soy garlic chicken - Huraideu chicken - plain fried chicken - Banban - half and half (usually half plain, half seasoned)

Tteokbokki (떡볶이)

Chewy rice cakes in spicy-sweet red sauce. A quintessential Korean street food. Often served with odeng (fish cakes) and twigim (fried items like vegetables, dumplings, or squid).

Mandu (만두)

Korean dumplings filled with meat and vegetables, served steamed (jjin mandu), fried (gun mandu), or in soup (mandu guk).

Street Food & Snacks

Hotteok (호떡)

Sweet griddled pancakes filled with brown sugar, cinnamon, and nuts. A popular winter street snack.

Bungeoppang (붕어빵)

Fish-shaped pastry filled with sweet red bean paste.

Eomuk / Odeng (어묵/오뎅)

Fish cakes on skewers, often served in broth at street stalls.

Gimbap (김밥)

As mentioned above, but commonly sold at street vendors and convenience stores as a snack or quick meal.

Sundae (순대)

Korean blood sausage made with glass noodles and barley. Despite the name, not related to the ice cream dessert!

Desserts & Sweet Treats

Bingsu (빙수)

Shaved ice dessert topped with sweet toppings. - Patbingsu - traditional version with sweet red beans, rice cakes, and condensed milk - Injeolmi bingsu - with roasted soybean powder - Modern versions with fruit, ice cream, and various toppings

Tteok (떡)

Korean rice cakes made from glutinous rice. - Songpyeon - crescent-shaped, filled with sweet ingredients - Injeolmi - coated in roasted soybean powder - Chapssaltteok - filled with sweet red bean paste

Yakgwa (약과)

Deep-fried honey cookies with a flower pattern, crispy outside and chewy inside.

Hodugwaja (호두과자)

Walnut-shaped pastries filled with sweet red bean paste and walnut pieces.

Beverages

Traditional Drinks

Soju (소주)

Korea’s most popular alcoholic beverage - a clear distilled spirit (16-20% ABV). Traditionally made from rice but now often from sweet potatoes. Served in shot glasses and consumed in one shot.

Drinking etiquette: - Pour drinks for others, not yourself - Use two hands when pouring or receiving from elders - Turn your head away when drinking in front of elders

Makgeolli (막걸리)

Milky, slightly sweet rice wine with a low alcohol content (6-8%). Traditional farmers’ drink.

Korean Tea (차)

  • Nokcha - green tea
  • Insam-cha - ginseng tea
  • Yujacha - yuzu citrus tea (often served as a hot sweet drink)
  • Boricha - roasted barley tea (commonly served as water in restaurants)

Modern Beverages

Banana Milk (바나나맛 우유)

Iconic Korean flavored milk in a distinctive yellow bottle. Available at all convenience stores.

Korean Coffee Culture

Korea has an extremely vibrant coffee culture with countless cafes. Popular chains include: - Ediya Coffee - affordable chain - Mega Coffee - known for large sizes - Paik’s Coffee - celebrity-owned chain

Regional Specialties

Seoul

  • Seolleongtang - ox bone soup
  • Kongnamul gukbap - bean sprout rice soup

Jeonju (전주)

  • Jeonju bibimbap - considered the best bibimbap in Korea
  • Kongnamul gukbap - bean sprout soup

Busan (부산)

  • Milmyeon - wheat noodles in cold broth
  • Ssiat hotteok - filled with seeds and nuts

Jeju Island (제주도)

  • Heuk dwaeji - black pork BBQ
  • Jeonbok juk - abalone porridge

Dietary Considerations

Vegetarian Options

Korean cuisine traditionally includes many vegetables, but many dishes contain meat or seafood-based broths and seasonings. Temple food (like you’ll experience at Geumsunsa) is completely vegetarian/vegan.

Look for: - Bibimbap (ask for no meat) - Japchae (vegetable version) - Kimchi jjigae (ask for no pork) - Dubu jorim - braised tofu

Note: Many kimchi varieties contain fish sauce or shrimp paste. Ask for “mukeunji” for older, milder kimchi that’s often vegetarian.

Spice Levels

Korean food can be quite spicy. If sensitive to spice, learn these phrases: - “Maepji ankae juseyo” (맵지 않게 주세요) - “Not spicy please” - “Maeweoyo” (매워요) - “It’s spicy”

Food Etiquette & Customs

Table Manners

  • Wait for the eldest person to start eating first
  • Don’t pick up your rice or soup bowl (unlike Japanese custom)
  • Use a spoon for rice and soup, chopsticks for side dishes
  • Don’t stick chopsticks upright in rice (funeral ritual)
  • It’s polite to finish everything on your plate

Dining Out

  • Banchan is unlimited - you can ask for more of any side dish
  • Call servers with “Yogi-yo!” (여기요!) or press the call button on the table
  • Tipping is not customary in Korea
  • Many restaurants specialize in just one dish
  • Split the bill is less common; one person typically pays

Korean BBQ Specific

  • Servers may cut the meat and grill for you
  • Don’t flip meat constantly - let it cook
  • Use the scissors to cut meat into bite-sized pieces
  • Wrap meat in lettuce with rice, garlic, and ssamjang

Food Vocabulary Quick Reference

Korean Pronunciation English
맛있어요 Masisseoyo Delicious
맵다 Maepda Spicy
계산서 주세요 Gyesanseo juseyo Check please
물 주세요 Mul juseyo Water please
고기 Gogi Meat
야채 Yachae Vegetables
고추장 Gochujang Red chili paste
쌈장 Ssamjang Thick dipping sauce
소주 Soju Korean alcohol

Restaurant Recommendations by Area

Insadong Area

  • Traditional tea houses
  • Samgyetang (ginseng chicken soup) restaurants
  • Korean dessert cafes

Itaewon

  • International cuisine
  • Fusion restaurants
  • Late-night dining options

Namdaemun Market

  • Street food stalls
  • Kalguksu (knife-cut noodles)
  • Hotteok stands

Myeongdong

  • Street food heaven
  • Korean fried chicken
  • Tteokbokki vendors

Tips for Food Adventurers

  1. Try temple food at Geumsunsa - it’s a unique vegetarian Buddhist cuisine experience
  2. Visit a traditional market like Namdaemun for authentic street food
  3. Do Korean BBQ properly - at least once, go to a mid-range restaurant where they grill for you
  4. Have a meal at a convenience store - sounds odd, but Korean convenience stores have surprisingly good instant noodles, kimbap, and triangular rice balls
  5. Try regional specialties - if visiting Jeonju, don’t miss the bibimbap
  6. Go to a pojangmacha - street tent bars for soju and snacks
  7. Experience a meal service (백반) - traditional meal with rice, soup, stew, and many banchan

Food Safety & Practical Notes

  • Tap water is safe to drink throughout Korea
  • Restaurants are generally very clean with high food safety standards
  • Most restaurants don’t accept reservations - expect to wait at popular spots
  • Many places close between lunch and dinner (around 3-5 PM)
  • Convenience stores are 24/7 and have ATMs, snacks, and basic necessities
  • Food delivery is incredibly advanced - apps like Coupang Eats and Baemin deliver almost anything

Must-Try Checklist

Before you leave Korea, make sure you’ve tried:

Enjoy your culinary adventure in Korea! 맛있게 드세요! (Masitge deuseyo - Enjoy your meal!)

Korea Trip Questions
